items.q: What is the relationship between the angle at the centre and the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc?
items.correct: The angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference.
items.misconception: The angles are equal because they are subtended by the same arc.
items.random_wrong: The angle at the centre is half the angle at the circumference.
items.why: Students intuitively expect 'same arc = same angle' and don't internalise that the central angle is double the inscribed angle.

items.misconception: (x + 3)^2 equals x^2 + 9 because squaring distributes over addition.
items.why: Students apply the (false) distributive rule (a + b)^n = a^n + b^n, missing the cross term from the actual expansion.

items.q: If the discriminant of a quadratic is negative, what does it tell you about real roots?
items.correct: There are no real roots — the graph does not cross the x-axis.
items.misconception: There is one repeated root — the curve just touches the x-axis.
items.why: Students confuse 'discriminant zero' (one repeated root) with 'discriminant negative' (no real roots) because both feel like 'unusual' cases.

items.q: The gradient of the line through (1, 2) and (4, 11) is:
items.why: Correct answer: 3. “1/3” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which line is perpendicular to y = 2x + 1?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: y = −x/2 + 3. “y = 2x − 1” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Find the radius of the circle x² + y² − 6x + 4y − 12 = 0.
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
